# ESTIMATION WITH SPANISH DATA {#sec-real}

With the procedure validated in @sec-simulat, this chapter constructs the observable series for the Spanish economy and applies the model to real data. This chapter first describes how each series is obtained and then applies Bayesian estimation to the Orsi-Raggi-Turino model on Spanish data for 1996–2024. Since the full sample includes the 2020 pandemic shock, which generates exceptional innovations, the pre-COVID sample (T=96) is adopted as the central estimation, and the full sample (T=116) is presented as a robustness exercise.

## Construction of the Observable Series

The model can incorporate up to eight observable series, but computational limitations required cutting this down to five. The two discarded were not the ones contributing the most information. These are social security contributions ($G^s_t$), which are implicit in the gross wage bill ($w^h_t = w_t(1+\tau^s_{ss})$); and the inspection probability ($p_t$), which shows little time variation in the Spanish administrative data.

### Private Consumption and Investment

Private consumption ($c_t$) and gross investment ($\text{inv}_t$) come from Eurostat's **harmonized Quarterly National Accounts**, which publishes the main demand aggregates for all member states. **Chain-linked volumes with base year 2010** (CLV) are used, expressing output in constant-efficiency units that strip out price effects, and the series are **seasonally and calendar adjusted**, which removes repetitive high-frequency patterns that the model would otherwise misinterpret as persistent technology shocks. Coverage is quarterly from 1995-Q1.

### GDP Deflator

Several series (wage compensation, tax revenue) are available only in nominal euros. To convert them into real terms comparable with the CLV series, the **GDP deflator** is constructed as the ratio between GDP at current prices and GDP in chain-linked volumes, both drawn from the same Eurostat table. It is not an observable of the model itself: it is used exclusively as an auxiliary price index.

### Employee Compensation

The gross wage bill ($w^h_t$) is obtained from the same Eurostat table, at current prices and seasonally adjusted. It is deflated using the implicit GDP deflator and normalized per capita to obtain the real series per working-age person.

### Corporate Tax and Personal Income Tax Revenue

Corporate Tax revenue ($G^c_t$) and Personal Income Tax revenue ($G^h_t$) come from IGAE's **Quarterly National Accounts** (*Non-financial Transactions of General Government*), which publishes quarterly tax revenue in national accounting terms.

A practical complication is that corporate tax exhibits **seasonal refunds concentrated in the first half of the year**: when firms settle their annual return, net quarterly revenue can be negative or very low. Since the observation equations work in logs, non-positive values break the Kalman filter. The solution adopted is a **four-quarter rolling sum**:

This transformation guarantees the series is always positive and removes seasonal variation while preserving the cyclical fluctuations the model aims to explain. It is applied equally to personal income tax for consistency. The first valid observation (with a sum of four complete quarters) is 1996-Q1.

### Tax Inspection Probability

The inspection probability $p_t$ is constructed as the ratio between the number of AEAT inspection actions and the number of active firms in Spain:

$$
p_t = \frac{\text{no. AEAT inspections}_t}{\text{no. firms (SA + SL) INE}_t}
$$

The numerator comes from the **AEAT's Annual Reports**, which publish yearly the volume of actions carried out by the Financial and Tax Inspection Department. The denominator comes from the INE's **Central Business Register (DIRCE)**, which publishes the census of public limited companies (SA) and limited liability companies (SL) active as of January 1 each year. For the years 1995–2000, DIRCE did not publish the SA+SL breakdown in its current format; for these years the denominator is imputed using the average of 2001–2003. The conversion from annual to quarterly assumes the probability is constant within the year.

The resulting series shows values around 2–3% throughout the whole period, with little cyclical variation. Precisely for this reason, this series is not included as a direct observable in the estimation. The steady-state parameter $p_{ss}$ is nonetheless estimated, identified through the model's general-equilibrium implications.

### Working-Age Population

The per capita normalization is obtained from Eurostat's **demographic table**, which publishes the population aged 15 to 64 as of January 1 each year for each member state. Being annual, it is linearly interpolated to quarterly frequency.

## Transformation and Time Coverage

### Observation Equations

Following the model's observation equations:

$$
\text{obs}_{x_t} = 100 \times \Delta \ln X^{pc}_t, \quad X \in \{c, \text{inv}, G^c, G^h, w^h\}
$$

where $X^{pc}_t$ is the real per capita series. The first log-difference is multiplied by 100 to express it in percentage points, units consistent with the steady-state value $\gamma_{pct}$.

### Coverage: Central Estimation (1996-Q1 to 2019-Q4)

The first valid period is **1996-Q1** because the four-quarter rolling sum for corporate/personal income tax requires four prior quarters (the 1995 observations are used as "burn-in"). The central estimation cuts the sample at **2019-Q4** (pre-COVID) to prevent the pandemic shock of the second quarter of 2020 from contaminating the structural estimates (see @sec-robustesa). The central sample comprises **T = 96 quarterly observations**. For robustness, the estimation is applied again over the full sample through **2024-Q4 (T = 116)** (@sec-robustesa). The complete summary of sources and transformations for each series can be found in @tbl-dades in the annex; the time evolution of each observable can be seen in @fig-obs-pairs.


## Parameters Fixed for the Spanish Economy {#sec-params-fixats}

The model distinguishes two types of parameters: *structural* ones (preferences and productivity), which are estimated using Bayesian methods because they cannot be observed directly, and *institutional* ones, which reflect the fiscal and penalty structure in force and can be approximated from administrative sources. In addition, two further parameters ($\sigma$ and $B_1$) are very weakly identified with the five available observables and are calibrated externally.

- **$\beta = 0.997$** (discount factor). Real yield on 10-year Spanish sovereign bonds deflated by the HICP 1995–2024: $\bar{r}_{quarterly} \approx 0.29\%$ → $\beta \approx 0.997$. The Italian paper uses 0.984, consistent with higher real rates over 1982–2006.

- **$\gamma_{ss} = 1.00$** (trend growth). Absorbed by $\gamma_{pct}$, which is estimated freely; simplifies detrending.

- **$B_0 = 84.7$** (total labor disutility). Calibrated so that $n^m_{ss}+n^u_{ss} = 0.19$, consistent with the employment rate adjusted for Spain. $B_0$ and $B_1$ have no direct quantitative interpretation on their own: only in relative terms do they convert units of consumption and labor into utils; their value is determined by the model's equilibrium conditions once all other parameters are fixed.

- **$B_1 = 300$** (additional disutility cost of informal labor). Calibrated; co-identified with $\phi$ through informal labor $n^u$, which is latent and not directly observable.

- **$\sigma = 0.99$** (inverse of the intertemporal elasticity of substitution). Calibrated; not identifiable without interest-rate data among the five available observables. As $\sigma \to 1$, the CRRA utility function converges to logarithmic utility in consumption; the value 0.99 is therefore equivalent to log-utility for all practical purposes. As a robustness exercise, the model could be re-estimated for higher values of $\sigma$, consistent with the microempirical evidence on the intertemporal elasticity of substitution [@guvenenReconcilingConflictingEvidence2006].

- **$\tau^c_{ss} = 0.25$** (effective corporate tax rate). The AEAT tax-due/tax-base ratio for 1996–2024 ranges between 22% and 28%; the ~25% average is adopted. The Italian paper uses 0.40.

- **$\tau^h_{ss} = 0.23$** (effective personal income tax rate). Employee's effective rate (~17%, Eurostat Tax-Benefit) plus employee-side social security contribution (~6.35%, TGSS) ≈ 23%. The Italian paper uses 0.35.

- **$\tau^s_{ss} = 0.23$** (employer social security contributions). Common contingencies rate under the General Regime: 23.6% nominal; effective rate on wage cost ~23%. The Italian paper uses 0.20.

- **$s = 1.70$** (penalty factor). LGT arts. 191–193: serious infringement penalty of 100% of the evaded tax due plus ~7.5% late-payment interest assuming detection after 2 years; a conservative value. The Italian paper uses 1.30.

Spain's effective tax rates are noticeably lower than Italy's ($\tau^c$: 25% vs. 40%; $\tau^h$: 23% vs. 35%), but the penalty factor is higher in Spain, which partly offsets the difference.

## Bayesian Estimation with Real Data {#sec-estimacio-real}

### Priors, Posteriors, and Convergence

The prior distributions follow Table 1 of @OrsiInformal. The three main adaptations relative to the original paper are: (1) $\gamma_{pct}$ is modeled as a truncated Normal centered at a quarterly 0.37% (the Spanish trend growth rate for 1995–2025, slightly above the Italian 0.23% for 1982–2006); (2) the AR(1) persistences are truncated above 0.97 for numerical stability; (3) the shock variances follow InverseGamma(3, 7.2×10⁻⁵), which centers the prior standard deviation at ~0.6%, in line with the original paper's prior.

@tbl-prioris-real reports the prior distributions, their means, and the posterior distribution results for the central estimation (1996-Q1 to 2019-Q4, T=96).

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[htbp]
\small
\centering
\caption{Prior distributions and posterior distribution results (central estimation, 1996-Q1 to 2019-Q4, T=96).}
\label{tbl-prioris-real}
\begin{tabular}{lp{4.3cm}ccp{3.6cm}c}
\toprule
 & Prior distribution & Prior mean & Post. mean & 95\% CI & $\hat{R}$ \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{6}{l}{\textbf{Structural}} \\[2pt]
$\alpha$          & Beta(1478, 796)                   & 0.650    & \textbf{0.670} & [0.653, 0.688] & 1.000 \\
$\delta_k$        & Beta(6.1, 236.7)                  & 0.025    & \textbf{0.017} & [0.013, 0.022] & 1.000 \\
$\alpha_u$        & Beta(1480, 766)                   & 0.659    & \textbf{0.638} & [0.620, 0.655] & 1.000 \\
$\phi$            & Gamma(400, 0.0025)                & 1.000    & \textbf{0.976} & [0.891, 1.066] & 1.000 \\
$\xi$             & Gamma(25, 0.04)                   & 1.000    & \textbf{1.984} & [1.697, 2.320] & 1.001 \\
$\gamma_{pct}$    & N$^+$(0.37, 0.20)                 & 0.370    & \textbf{0.275} & [0.156, 0.398] & 1.001 \\
$p_{ss}$          & Beta(8.7, 281.3)                  & 0.030    & \textbf{0.026} & [0.011, 0.045] & 1.000 \\[6pt]
\multicolumn{6}{l}{\textbf{Persistences}} \\[2pt]
$\rho_a$          & Beta(12, 3)$^{\leq 0.97}$         & 0.800    & \textbf{0.861} & [0.767, 0.932] & 1.000 \\
$\rho_b$          & Beta(12, 12)$^{\leq 0.97}$        & 0.500    & \textbf{0.519} & [0.313, 0.734] & 1.001 \\
$\rho_c$          & Beta(7.2, 0.8)$^{\leq 0.97}$      & 0.900    & \textbf{0.946} & [0.912, 0.968] & 1.001 \\
$\rho_s$          & Beta(7.2, 0.8)$^{\leq 0.97}$      & 0.900    & \textbf{0.933} & [0.911, 0.952] & 1.000 \\
$\rho_h$          & Beta(7.2, 0.8)$^{\leq 0.97}$      & 0.900    & \textbf{0.967} & [0.957, 0.970] & 1.000 \\
$\rho_{\xi_h}$    & Beta(13.8, 9.2)$^{\leq 0.97}$     & 0.600    & \textbf{0.600} & [0.397, 0.790] & 1.001 \\
$\rho_x$          & Beta(12, 3)$^{\leq 0.95}$         & 0.800    & \textbf{0.930} & [0.892, 0.949] & 1.000 \\
$\rho_p$          & Beta(12, 3)$^{\leq 0.97}$         & 0.800    & \textbf{0.799} & [0.569, 0.950] & 1.001 \\[6pt]
\multicolumn{6}{l}{\textbf{Shock Variances}} \\[2pt]
$\sigma^2_a$      & IG(3, 0.000072)                   & 0.000036 & \textbf{0.0001} & [0.0001, 0.0001] & 1.001 \\
$\sigma^2_b$      & IG(3, 0.000072)                   & 0.000036 & \textbf{0.0000} & ---$^2$         & 1.000 \\
$\sigma^2_c$      & IG(3, 0.000072)                   & 0.000036 & \textbf{0.0080} & [0.0060, 0.0107] & 1.001 \\
$\sigma^2_s$      & IG(3, 0.000072)                   & 0.000036 & \textbf{0.0174} & [0.0126, 0.0238] & 1.000 \\
$\sigma^2_h$      & IG(3, 0.000072)                   & 0.000036 & \textbf{0.0005} & [0.0003, 0.0006] & 1.001 \\
$\sigma^2_{\xi_h}$& IG(3, 0.000072)                   & 0.000036 & \textbf{0.0000} & [0.0000, 0.0001] & 1.000 \\
$\sigma^2_x$      & IG(3, 0.000072)                   & 0.000036 & \textbf{0.0000} & ---$^2$         & 1.000 \\
$\sigma^2_p$      & IG(3, 0.000072)                   & 0.000036 & \textbf{0.0000} & [0.0000, 0.0001] & 1.000 \\
\bottomrule
\multicolumn{6}{l}{\footnotesize $^2$ CI = [0.0000, 0.0000]; inactive shock (posterior collapsed at the prior's lower bound).}
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```


 The estimation was carried out with 3 independent chains of 3,000 iterations each (1,000 adaptation + 2,000), giving a total of 6,000 posterior samples. Convergence is excellent for all parameters. The $\hat{R}$ statistic does not exceed **1.001** for any of the 23 estimated parameters. Effective sample size is good, and the traceplots show mixing across the three chains with no discernible patterns. The prior-versus-posterior plots, traceplots, and full corner plots are found in @sec-annex-real.

## Results {#sec-resultats-real}

### Structural Parameters

The model's structural parameters show an uneven degree of identification from the five observables. Best identified by the Spanish data are $\alpha$, $\delta_k$, $\alpha_u$, $\xi$, and $\gamma_{pct}$; those that remain close to the prior are $\phi$, $p_{ss}$, and $B_1$ (calibrated).

**Formal labor elasticity ($\alpha = 0.670$).** The posterior lies slightly above the prior (0.650), within a very narrow 95% CI of [0.653, 0.688].

**Capital depreciation rate ($\delta_k = 0.017$).** The posterior shifts substantially below the prior (prior: mean=0.025, posterior: 0.017). An effective quarterly depreciation of 1.7% corresponds to 6.8% annually, below the 10% typical of standard calibrations. This divergence may reflect that the model, in explaining observed investment through the volatility of fiscal and productivity shocks, requires lower depreciation to reconcile investment flows with the capital accumulation implicit in the Spanish data.

**Total labor supply disutility ($\xi = 1.984$).** This is the largest and economically most relevant shift. The posterior (mean 1.98, CI [1.70, 2.32]) sits well above the prior (mean 1.00). $\xi$ governs the convexity of the disutility of *total* hours worked ($n^m_t+n^u_t$) and is therefore the inverse Frisch elasticity of aggregate labor supply: a higher $\xi$ means households are less willing to work more or less in total when incentives change. A posterior nearly double the prior implies that Spanish households' total labor supply is substantially more rigid than assumed a priori (Frisch elasticity of total hours $\approx 1/1.98 \approx 0.50$, about half the prior value of 1.0). Because $\phi$ — the parameter governing the formal-informal reallocation margin specifically — stays close to its prior (see below), this rigidity in total hours means that, when a fiscal shock alters the net wage differential between the formal and informal sectors, households cannot easily absorb it by working more or less overall; instead, nearly all of the adjustment is channeled into reallocating existing hours between the two sectors. It is this rigidity of total hours, rather than a directly larger formal-informal substitution elasticity, that explains why fiscal shocks translate so strongly into swings in the underground share [@chettyAreMicroMacro2011].

**Steady-state inspection probability ($p_{ss} = 0.026$).** The estimated value is close both to the prior (0.030) and to the AEAT administrative data (~2–3%), confirming that the prior calibration was adequate.

### Shock Persistences

The persistences of the fiscal shocks ($\rho_c = 0.946$, $\rho_s = 0.933$, $\rho_h = 0.967$) are all above 0.90, indicating that changes in Spanish fiscal policy are highly persistent: a change to corporate tax, social security contributions, or personal income tax takes between 10 and 30 quarters to dissipate by half. This is consistent with the institutional reality: Spain's effective tax rates were very stable during the period of euro entry (1996–2007), and subsequent adjustments were gradual and often permanent.

TFP persistence ($\rho_a = 0.861$) is notably lower than the fiscal persistences, suggesting that technology cycles revert faster than fiscal ones in the Spanish economy. In contrast, the preference shock ($\rho_b = 0.519$, CI [0.313, 0.734]) shows the lowest persistence and the widest posterior range, indicating weak identification from the five available observables.

### Shock Volatilities

A notable result is the marked heterogeneity in the estimated volatilities. Four of the model's eight shocks turn out to be practically inactive in the Spanish data: $\sigma_b$, $\sigma_{\xi_h}$, $\sigma_x$, and $\sigma_p$ all converge to the lower bound of the InverseGamma prior. This is not a model failure but an identification result: the observed fluctuations in consumption, investment, corporate tax revenue, personal income tax revenue, and wages are explained without needing preference shocks, informal-supply shocks, investment-technology shocks, or inspection shocks. The model is parsimonious in the number of active shocks.

The four active shocks show highly divergent volatilities.

- **$\sigma_a = 1.0\%$**: formal production technology is stable; most fluctuations are attributed to fiscal shocks, not TFP.

- **$\sigma_c = 8.9\%$**: high volatility of corporate tax disturbances, reflecting the sensitivity of corporate revenue to the business cycle and to tax reforms.

- **$\sigma_s = 13.2\%$**: the largest volatility, associated with social security contribution shocks. Spain has experienced substantial changes in employer contributions over 1996–2019, and the cyclical component of contributions is amplified by variability in employment.

- **$\sigma_h = 2.2\%$**: moderate volatility for personal income tax shocks, consistent with the greater stability of marginal income tax rates compared with corporate tax.

## Robustness Analysis: COVID versus Pre-COVID Sample {#sec-robustesa}

To assess the sensitivity of the results to the 2020 pandemic shock, the estimation is applied again over the full sample (1996-Q1 to 2024-Q4, T=116). @tbl-robustesa compares the key parameters between the two estimations.

The parameters that are **qualitatively stable** across the two samples — $\alpha$, $\alpha_u$, $\phi$, $p_{ss}$, $\rho_h$, $\sigma^2_c$ — confirm that the model's main conclusions do not depend on including the pandemic. The parameters **sensitive to COVID** point to three distinct mechanisms:

1. **$\delta_k$: 0.017 → 0.032 (+88%).** The massive collapse in investment in 2020-Q2 (−40% quarterly) and capital underutilization during the lockdowns translate into a very high effective depreciation in those quarters. When the model tries to explain the consumption and investment series with a single, constant depreciation shock, it calibrates permanent depreciation upward to accommodate that extreme observation.

2. **$\rho_a$: 0.861 → 0.949 (+10 pp).** The slow post-COVID recovery (2021–2022) is interpreted by the model as a highly persistent negative technology shock. This inflation of TFP persistence is an artifact: with the pre-COVID sample, TFP shows normal recovery cycles (1998, 2003, 2013), and the posterior is 0.86.

3. **$\sigma^2_s$ and $\sigma^2_h$: +36% and +140% respectively.** 2020 recorded a 30% drop in employer contributions (furlough schemes, "ERTEs") and a 25% drop in personal income tax, variations far above any previous fluctuation. The model inflates the permanent volatilities to accommodate these exceptional observations, overestimating the structural volatility of the fiscal shocks.

|  | Pre-COVID (T=96) | 95% CI | COVID (T=116) | 95% CI |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\alpha$ | 0.670 | [0.653, 0.688] | 0.664 | [0.646, 0.682] |
| $\delta_k$ | **0.017** | [0.013, 0.022] | **0.032** | [0.023, 0.043] |
| $\alpha_u$ | 0.638 | [0.620, 0.655] | 0.644 | [0.626, 0.662] |
| $\phi$ | 0.976 | [0.891, 1.066] | 0.949 | [0.870, 1.031] |
| $\xi$ | 1.984 | [1.697, 2.320] | 2.144 | [1.816, 2.517] |
| $\gamma_{pct}$ | **0.275** | [0.156, 0.398] | **0.335** | [0.186, 0.491] |
| $p_{ss}$ | 0.026 | [0.011, 0.045] | 0.027 | [0.013, 0.046] |
| $\rho_a$ | **0.861** | [0.767, 0.932] | **0.949** | [0.911, 0.969] |
| $\rho_c$ | 0.946 | [0.912, 0.968] | 0.932 | [0.894, 0.964] |
| $\rho_s$ | 0.933 | [0.911, 0.952] | 0.951 | [0.928, 0.968] |
| $\rho_h$ | 0.967 | [0.957, 0.970] | 0.966 | [0.955, 0.970] |
| $\sigma^2_c$ | 0.0080 | [0.006, 0.011] | 0.0084 | [0.007, 0.011] |
| $\sigma^2_s$ | **0.0174** | [0.013, 0.024] | **0.0236** | [0.017, 0.031] |
| $\sigma^2_h$ | 0.0005 | [0.0003, 0.0006] | **0.0012** | [0.0009, 0.0015] |


: Comparison of posteriors between the pre-COVID estimation (T=96) and the full sample (T=116). {#tbl-robustesa tbl-colwidths="[10,22,22,22,22]"}

For all these reasons, **the pre-COVID estimation (T=96) is the reference estimation** of this work. The COVID sample provides useful information as a sensitivity exercise, confirming that the deep structural parameters are robust to the inclusion of the pandemic.

## Comparison with the Results of Orsi, Raggi, and Turino (2014) {#sec-comparacio-italia}

The Orsi-Raggi-Turino model was originally estimated on Italian data for 1982–2006, with seven observables. The work closest to the Spanish context is @herranzTaxEvasionFiscal2023, which estimates a similar DSGE model with tax evasion on Spanish data for 1985–2015 and obtains a share of public debt attributable to the underground economy of roughly 23%. A direct parameter-by-parameter comparison between the present estimation and @OrsiInformal is limited by three factors: (1) different sample periods, (2) a different number of observables (seven vs. five), and (3) different calibrated parameters, especially the tax rates and the penalty factor. Nonetheless, the comparison remains interesting and of economic value.

### Structural Parameters

The production elasticities $\alpha$ and $\alpha_u$ show similar values across the two economies, stable across European economies of similar income levels. The most notable difference is $\xi$, the parameter governing the disutility (and hence the inverse Frisch elasticity) of total hours worked: the value estimated for Spain ($\xi \approx 2.0$) is roughly double the paper's prior ($\xi \approx 1.0$), meaning Spanish households' total labor supply is markedly less elastic than assumed. Because $\phi$ — the parameter that directly governs the formal-informal reallocation margin — remains close to its prior, this rigidity in total hours means that changes in incentives are absorbed almost entirely by reallocating existing hours between the formal and informal sectors, rather than by households working more or less overall. This interpretation is consistent with research on the duality of the Spanish labor market, which documents a high share of temporary contracts and the ease with which workers move between the formal and informal sectors depending on the cycle.

### Persistences

Spanish TFP persistence ($\rho_a = 0.861$) is lower than what the paper obtains for Italy (around 0.90–0.93). This difference reflects sharper Spanish growth cycles and faster TFP reversals: Spain had very rapid expansions (1996–2007) followed by sudden downturns (2008–2013), whereas the Italian cycle of 1982–2006 was less pronounced.
